speaker: interviewer
text: And the survivor is Henry Kreisel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Just to go back-step for a moment, while in [INAUDIBLE], in England, I started a correspondence course for a high school diploma. And the same company in Britain also had a branch in-- in-- in Jerusalem of the correspondence course. And I continued with that, the correspondence course, after I got out of the-- out of the Air Force. And I took several subjects. And-- and then in 19-- and then I got a job, several different jobs in furniture factories. Then I got a job in 1951. I got a job as a teacher, a shop-- as a shop teacher in [INAUDIBLE] school in Tel Aviv. And-- and I was also doing this-- this high school correspondence. And then it got to a point I wanted to go, a little adventure, see my family. I haven't seen any of my family, other than my sister, since 1939. And I had family, those who weren't killed, were in America-- uncles, and aunts, and cousins in America. I wanted to get to know my family. I wanted to complete my education. I was into education. And I wanted a bit of adventure, life adventure. You know, I was getting a little bit-- I wanted a little adventure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in February-- February, of 1954, I went to-- I got an affidavit from my aunt to go to United States. On the way there, I stopped off-- it was by boat then-- I stopped off in England for a few months, about three months, to visit with my sister and so on. Where was her sister at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was, at that point, still in that little village near-- down south. But she was not happy there. Things were not going that well there for her. And I wanted to get out-- her get-- get out of there. And with the help of friends, of Mrs. Rosenfelder, who was running this-- this hostel-- hostel, she got her a job through a friend of hers-- as a-- she was trained as a secretary, a steno-typist then. And she got her a job, and she got her a place to live. So she was then able to move to London, which was very important for her. And-- because that was a bad place for her to be with. The father-- the man had died, and the woman was so possessive. And so it was a bad place, it got to be a bad-- of course her life was saved, I mean. But it got to be a bad place to live. We got her out, thank god, with the help of other people. I couldn't have gotten her out myself. And I've had a good fortune in life. There was always somebody there to help me, either give me a helping hand or to give me helping advice. Always throughout my life, I've had this good fortune. And-- and to this day, the people I can talk to about advice and so on. And-- so, when I got to-- so then I'm in America. I got here. And I stayed with one of my uncles and aunts. And I went-- the second day was here, the following day was here, I went to the-- the unemployment office, for my card, for my social security card, and for a job. And they used to give-- there was plenty of jobs available, back in-- back in '54-- in the beginning of '54.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in America were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brooklyn, New York. And they gave me a job to a factory. And within a week, I was working in a furniture factory, making $1 an hour, $1.05 an hour. That was big for me, $1.05-- $1.05 an hour, 40 hours, I mean that was big money. And I was very happy about this. And then I got drafted. While on the boat, getting off the boat, I had-- I go to different tables, different writing. There was a table there with-- for drafting people. That was back in '54. There was the war, and there was a problem with offshore islands, Quemoy and Matsu, in offshore islands from China, you know, possibility of another war there. So they're drafting people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, I don't want to go. I've just got here. I want to get to know my relatives. So of course, I spoke fluent English. Because I was raised in England, you know. So he said, listen, my friend. If you don't want to go to the army, you can take the same back-- boat back where you came from. You know, that's what he told me. So I said, OK. So I registered. And a couple-- a few weeks later, about 10 weeks later, in July-- I got there in April, middle of April. In July '54, I was drafted into the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, again, a lot of things in life are [NON-ENGLISH], are luck. We think good things happen, because we are so big and smart and strong. And the army was the best thing that ever happened to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because I got to go to Japan. I was stationed in Japan. And I-- I was-- I worked for the AG Corps, Adjutant General Corps, which was running the port of Yokohama, which were bringing-- which was adjacent to-- to Tokyo, where they took up part of the port, which supplied the Americans in Japan with food, and ammunition, and clothes, and everything what the American army needs. There's a lot of things an army needs, eggs, you know, these powder eggs, and the milk, and Coca-Colas. You know, there's lot of things, besides ammunition, that an army needs, and especially American-- a luxury army like America, you know, where they have all kinds of things and facilities. And I was-- and then the section I was in, we ran what they call billets for officers and civilians. Soldiers live in barracks, you know, but officers live in billets, you know. And then we had requisitioned Japanese hotels, were requisitioned by the US Army, where these people were billeted. And some of them, they also built themselves. You know, but there were some-- so I was in charge of a couple of them. Well, I was in charge technically. I know nothing about hotels, you know. I was a guy who hadn't even, you know, finished high school really. You know, I did not even have my high school diploma really. I was still working on it, although I'd been a teacher, a shop teacher, in Israel, and had some education. But technically, I hadn't even--

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, so I got this hotel, being in charge of a couple of these hotels. But there was Japanese workers under me, managers and other workers under me, who really did the job. But they had-- in all American facilities, you have to have American staff present, in case something goes wrong. You know, they got-- the-- the local people go on strike or whatever it was. So I mean being a student, I made it my business to learn the job. I went to classes. They had-- they had classes in the evenings, apartment of the-- for-- for soldiers. And I got a sort of an equivalency high school diploma. While you you were in Japan?

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was in the army, I got an equivalency-- there was a special army section which was doing it. And I got equivalency high school diploma. I took a couple of also college-level courses. During-- I can't think of the name of it-- but it was a section of the army which does that. And-- and-- so-- so then I was-- time to be discharged, two years later it was time to be discharged. And I didn't want to stay on in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was 1956?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was already 1956. I got in '54. I was only-- I was drafted. So I was only allowed to be discharged. But they're trying to renew you and all that. And it was very tempting. I had a great life in Japan. But I knew that I wanted other things. I knew I wasn't going to marry a Japanese girl. I want to marry a Jewish woman. And I want to get an education, and I wanted to be reunited with the sister. That couldn't happen in Japan. So I had to leave all this paradise there. It was a paradise for American soldiers. And I left. I came back, came back, and got a job at the post office at night, it was mainly. And I went to-- in the daytime I went to a high school, here-- here in New York-- in New York. I can't think of the name right now. It will come back to me while we're talking, and got that regular high school diploma in February-- February 1957. And I also entered college in February '57, City College in New York. Because you had-- for City College, you had to have a regular high school diploma. My-- my equivalency didn't count. They wouldn't count that, you know. And so I had to get a regular high school diploma. And I got that. Based upon some-- they transferred the stuff that I did in Israel through the correspondence course, they transferred that. But I had to take a couple-- I had to take American history and civics. There were several things I had to take-- so-- in the high school, and a few subjects to get my high school diploma. And I got that. And then I got a job, a different-- different job, different Sunday jobs. And then in 1959-- I'm going to go through it quickly, because you're running out of time-- I got a job as a Hebrew teacher, [? Tamil ?] Torah, afternoon schools. Kids go to school in daytime, and go to school to-- to a Hebrew school in the afternoon. It's not on a very high level. And I was able to handle that, based partly on my-- the foundation I got in that group home. And also I knew Hebrew pretty well. And I was learning on my own subsequently, and I was able to be a Hebrew teacher. And I did that from 1959 through 1964-- till 1963, till 1960-- I think it was 1960-- 1963. And it was '64, I think it was '64, 1964. It was five years. And then I came back to London. And I was teaching-- I was teaching in Toledo, Ohio four years, and one year in Syracuse, New York as a Hebrew teacher. And then I wanted to get back to New York. Because there was not much Jewish life in these places, and I got my bachelor's degree in '54 from University of Toledo, going to school-- going to school there in daytime, teaching-- teaching Jewish children, adults in afternoon and at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I got my-- then I went back to New York. And not mentioning small jobs I had, selling, and this and that, you know. I got a job at the Department-- New York State Department-- New York City Department of Social Services. There are plenty of jobs available. They were building up a new-- a new welfare center every week, then back in the-- back in those days in '64, almost every week. It was biggest industry in New York, welfare, you know. So I got a job immediately. And I went to school at night. And I got a master's in the-- next following five years, I got a master's in people personnel service from City College. And I was-- and I-- and I transferred then from the city-- from the city social services, after a couple of years I was-- I was already a supervisor for the city-- I got transferred to the state.

speaker: interviewee
text: The state was a superior job. It was a superior job. The state and other agencies used the city for training ground, you know. So by the time a guy got a few years at the city, a couple of years, he was eligible to take exams for probation officer or social services for the state. You know, it was a background for the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got back to the state. And I've been there altogether about 27 and 1/2 years with social services in the state, city and state. And then I retired at age 62 in 19-- April-- April 1991. And-- and so essentially, essentially I am-- as you look around, you see books, and not just books I have [? Shoah ?] books which I read, which I bought in college for myself to study, and I'm still a student today. I still go to classes and go to lectures, and study on my own.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- Jewish and secular, it's primarily Jewish, but also secular subjects. And-- and-- and also I bought a computer, which I took, for example, a couple of courses on practicing with and collecting information. Because I plan to do some writing, with God's help. And-- and that's pretty well-- and my sister, I brought my sister to America in 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she in the meantime was working as a-- as a secretary. And then she joined the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- what do they call it? The-- the-- the people did the-- it's private. They have it here also. The Blue Cross-- not Blue Cross, the Red Cross. She joined the Red Cross in England, got to like it. So she moved, and got into nursing. She got into nursing, because of her experiences in the Red Cross, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she became a nurse in '54. And she's been a nurse ever since. And when she came, and I brought her to America, she had a-- she had a registered nurse and her you know, license, and also-- they also used to, like midwives, midwives license. They used to do that in England. And she got a job immediately. And she's been working ever since in America. And for the last 27 years almost, she's been working in-- for the city, Department of Health. And she's due to retire shortly. And we are in touch. We're in touch. We're-- talk on the phone all the time. And-- and we get in touch. Last-- yesterday, I took out her out for lunch. Sometimes she stops by here. We have dinner together. We're very close. We are very close. We're in touch, and America has been good to both of us. It's a good country. But for the last few years, I've been beginning to worry about America. I'm-- I'm very gung ho in America. But I'm beginning to worry about America. Because some of the things which are happening here may lead us to the same situation as all other empires were led to. We're going down because of lowered-- lowered levels of standards of morality. That will bring-- Russia and China will never bring down America. America will be brought down by-- by the lowered standards of morality. And-- and we have all this addiction, homosexuality, addiction, crime, and all these other things which are destroying America. Which I'm very sorry, because it's a good country. And I'm concerned about that, and hope there can be turnarounds. Because in part, Israel also depends upon America. It isn't that the Jews, they were so big and so strong that they didn't get help from anybody. They had a lot of help. Without America, Israel wouldn't be there today, and other places. And so America does a lot of good things for the world. Maybe like-- like-- like Netanyahu said, Israel will be financially dependent for the new program he has for the next few years. But a lot of other countries who need American help, both financially and technically. And if America goes down, so does those other countries in the world go down. And so we have some pretty-- some very-- hopefully there will be a turnaround so that America will remain strong. And incidentally, America-- and I was just reading it recently-- was built on Torah philosophy, the original fathers. They were even considering using Hebrew as a language, as the national language rather than English. And they also built on the principles of the B'nai Noah. B'nai Noah are non-Jews. But the Torah gives commandments, seven aspects of commandments and sub commandments, of what every human being in the world should do. There are laws what every Jew should do. And then there are seven mitzvots of B-nail Noah, seven commandments, and they account the, you know, details for B'nai Noah of all humanity. All humanity must not rob and kill, must maintain courts of justice. And America was built on this system. And that is perhaps the closeness we have to America. America essentially, the essence of America is still a godly country. And that's why we have to be strong, be really very strong in keeping it that. Because there are a lot of forces which are undermining America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that why you gave this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: The reason I gave an interview, I saw that book, that-- that movie, And it was the greatest movie I ever saw. And what was again, the guy who produced it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Steven Spielberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Steven Spielberg, and I read that Steven Spielberg is doing this program-- is doing this program. And I had confidence in Steven Spielberg. Because Steven Spielberg was a big man just before doing this movie. He could have done any other movie, which would be big. You know, all his movies are big. He didn't have to do this. But he did this. He wanted to, because his own-- his also personal development, what he is, what he has become as a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- and-- and also we see all these movements now of counterculture, which are saying, there was-- the Jews are making it all up. There were no concentration camps. Maybe there were a couple hundred Jews killed. Some were by accident. But there were no concentration camps. What? 6 million Jews got killed? It's impossible. It could never happen. It's all lies.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this counterculture is not only among low-class people, it's been going around universities. Some people even, respectable or-- or officially respectable people, of course they're swine, Nazi swine at heart. Because they cannot admit what this kind of-- that would-- and some are Christo-Nazis, Christians who want to destroy the Jewish religion. And when the Jews don't go along, they prepare to the Jews, destroy the Jews. But that whole Nazi culture is based upon Christo-Nazism. Where they have all this-- there's all this ideology against Jews. That's what Hitler was able to work on. Although he himself went to a-- went to a Catholic high school, from which he never graduated. But that's where he got all these ideas of the Jewish being the anti-god, the Christ killers, and the anti-god. That's where he got his all his ideas from regarding Jews. In the town where he lived, he didn't know any Jews. There only a few town-- there were only a few Jews in the town where he lived. They were assimilated. He never saw a Jew with a-- with a beard and payot. He never saw one before until he moved to Vienna, where he saw Jews from Poland who were pious Jews. But that's where he got his ideas from, is straight from the Catholic church. And the guy who was captured by, and hanged in Israel, the-- the one-- the mass murderer--

speaker: interviewer
text: Eichmann.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eichmann went to the same school. They had the same history teacher. That's where they got the ideas from, that Catholic school. And there were such Catholic schools all over the place, and other even Protestant schools, but not as bad as the Catholics. And that's where all these people had this inborn hatred against Jews already, ready for the Nazis to work on and utilize. And now this is being denied.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're 50 years down the road, and the world has not changed that much. The world has changed somewhat. But I believe technically a Holocaust is still possible. I'm watching this stuff on television, what's going on-- and what's the town in Yugoslavia? Bosnia and Sarajevo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bosnia, Serbia, Bosnia, where they have cleansing, ethnic cleansing, where they kill people. They rape the women. They kill people, mass graves. It's still going on today. And fortunately, the UN is having hearings about this. These things are going on in Africa, one in Africa, where we were-- where we were there. The Americans intervened about a year ago, in Burundi-- what's the town next to Burundi, that state there, where the Tutsis killed the Hutus, over a million were killed, two different tribes; in Somalia, where people are all the same, they part of the Arab League. They speak the same language, have the same religion. But people in this village under this chief, must kill the people in the village and other chief and another chief, the same people. And there are no Christians or Jews there. This is Muslim people. And they're killing each other because they live in different villages. So that stuff which went on in Germany is still going on today, maybe not on the same world war scale, not as organized. They don't have concentration camps with ovens, and you know, and gas chambers. But a lot of killing can be done by rifles, and by knives, and by hatchets. And by-- you know, by axes. You know, you don't have to throw spears. You don't have to have a sophisticated army to kill a lot of people, as these various places are proving.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I-- I-- I think that's what I wanted to-- that there should be a record and of different people. So they can't say it's all imaginary, even the little bit that I'm doing. They can't say, this guy imagined it all. I do have a good imagination. But I don't-- not good enough to imagine all this, all the experience I had in my life. So that's why I did it. And I think Spielberg and his group are doing a great job, and there a lot of things have to be done, and a lot of things have to be done, particularly in building up.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] should be a Jewish state. It should not be a Nazi Arab state. We'd be in the same position again under the Arabs that we were under the Nazis. And the Arabs, make no mistake, hate the Jews as much as the Nazis ever did. And people say, no, no, no. It's not true. It's not true. They-- they're liberals. Even now in Saudi Arabia, some poor guy steals-- steals something, a tire or a jacket, he gets his hand cut off, or the second time he gets his foot cut off. But the-- but the-- the-- the so-called sheikhs, the so-called the princes and kings, they are-- are able to steal anywhere from $1 million to $10 billion. And the money that-- from the oils, which God gave to all the Arabs, he didn't give it just to a few Arabs, to the Arab sheiks who are strong enough to have an army to suppress the others. And that's why we saw that-- that bombing in-- in-- in Saudi Arabia a few weeks ago. And as sure as I'm sitting here talking to you, as sure as that, I'm sure that in the next few years those princes will be overthrown, and they will all be killed, even if they run to America. They already have billions of dollars stashed away in America, and Britain, France, and in-- in Japan, and all over the world, billions of dollars. Airplanes on the ready to take off, but they-- all of them, and they're deliberately paying money to-- to terrorist organizations to attack the Jews. And they even pay money in the Philippines to the Muslims there. Because they want the focus to be on others. They want all these organizations to focus on other people, not on them. Because the biggest criminals, the ones who have done the Arabs the most harm, are these sheikhs, these Arab princes and royal-- self-styled royalty errand boy royalties, who are stealing what belongs to the Arab people. All that money, the billions and trillions of dollars which have been transferred to the Arab countries without the Arabs having to lift a finger, it was foreign countries who found the oil, foreign countries who brought up to the ground, who distributed it. Without lifting a finger, they got-- God gave them trillions of dollars. And if that money would be distributed among the 22 Arab states, of the Arab League, they can all live decent lives, have all the hospitals, have all the schools, and all have decent lives. Whereas many of them still live in abject poverty and-- because of these Arab sheikhs. And America, has to think twice what's going on-- what's going on, the blowing up of the-- of these soldiers there. We are placing our hopes in these-- in these errand boys, kings. And we see how they hate, not only hate Jews, but hate Christians. It's very fundamental. And there will be fundamentalism in the Muslim states, within Arab states for the next several hundred years, fundamentalism. And if anybody thinks otherwise, that plane which blew up the other day-- what's the name, the TWA plane that blew up a day or two ago-- that may have been from a bomb placed, or a rocket placed by a radical-- a radical-- a radical from an Arab state. And America knows it. And if people think, like these Marxist atheists, like Rabin and Peres thought, oh yeah, we'll all sing the-- we'll all thing the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- national, you know, the Communist national together. We'll all be happy together in a state. They might think that was the idealism. The Arabs don't think that. They were just using-- going along with Rabin and Peres. They were using these traitors to the Jews for their benefit. They had no respect for them. They respect Netanyahu a lot more, although they don't like him, than they ever, ever could have respected a traitor to their own people. Because the Arabs have-- have-- also have their own dignity and their own honor. And they know a traitor, and disrespect him. And this is-- and I'm very happy that recently that there was a change in government and the Marxist atheists were thrown out. And now we have a chance again. Had it not been so, Israel could have been destroyed. The Jews in Israel would have had to be evacuated within the next 10 years. And there would have been an Arab, an Arab-Judeo state. Some Jews would have remained. Some of the [? yeshivas ?] would have remained still. There's contribution from foreign countries, but they would have been in the subservient positions to Arabs, as Jews have been traditional to Arabs. We have lived with Arabs. We have lived with Arabs since the destruction of the first temple 500 BC until now. The Jews were-- when we were in Persia and in Iran, and Iraq, and Syria, we have lived with Arabs for 2,500 years. So we can live with Arabs. But we couldn't live under Marxist atheism, which talks about the pro-Zionist period, and was wanting the destruction of the Jewish religion, and the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much.
